proposed_from.topic: Sharp identification of the long-run average treatment effect in a discrete-time panel where treatment is reversible (non-absorbing) and dynamic spillovers between consecutive treatment spells are bounded by an explicit scalar parameter: a closed-form sequence of nested g-formulas characterizing the identified set as a function of the spillover bound, recovering the Robins (1986) g-formula point identification when the bound is zero and the unrestricted Manski-style bounds when the bound is infinite, with a sharpness witness in the form of two extremal joint laws that saturate each face of the identified set; extends Han-Yao-Hsu (2024) absorbing-treatment dynamic identification to the reversible regime explicitly left open in their conclusion
proposed_from.novelty_justification: {"repo_axis":"Closest in-catalogue prior art remains pid_dynamic_iv_compliance_v2/v3, pid_long_term_surrogate_v1, and pid_did_anticipation_bounded_v1. Those banked proposals concern retained instrument memory, bounded transition/coupling witnesses, or finite DID support-function aggregation; none states an observable finite-window endpoint-diameter criterion for convergence of reversible-spell finite sharp intervals to a stationary long-run identified set, nor a block-switching reversible-spell witness against stationary summary.","published_axis":"Closest published comparators are Han2021 for finite dynamic sequence identification, ChernozhukovFernandezValHahnNewey2013 and JunLeeShin2016 for sharp panel bounds under imposed time-homogeneity/stationarity, Schneider1993/RockafellarWets1998 for generic compact-convex stability, and KalouptsidiKitamuraLimaSouzaRodrigues2020 plus DuarteFinkelsteinKnoxMummoloShpitser2024 for finite sharp-bounds workflows. The epsilon-gap is an iff finite-window endpoint-diameter frontier for reversible-spell long-run sharp bounds, and a named warning that horizon-by-horizon sharp endpoints cannot be summarized by one stationary interval when endpoint cycling persists."}
seed_list: Prior work Robins1986 would predict that dynamic sustained-regime means are point-identified by the g-formula under full longitudinal assumptions. Our seed conjectures that a scalar adjacent-spell Lipschitz envelope is the sharp partial-identification bridge away from that point model, because reversible on-off spells create a finite metric-extension problem rather than an absorbing-regime recursion.

Prior work DiemerShiSwanson2024 would imply that assumption-free sustained dynamic intervention bounds remain wide when latent histories are unrestricted. Our seed conjectures that bounded adjacent-spell carryover strictly nests those bounds, because repeated reversible spells create observed support points that act as Lipschitz anchors for off-path histories.

Prior work ChernozhukovFernandezValHahnNewey2013 would predict sharp panel bounds from time-homogeneity and state-dependence restrictions. Our seed conjectures that sharpness can instead be certified by McShane-Whitney extremal laws on a spell-boundary metric, because the binding faces are metric-extension faces rather than stationarity faces.

Prior work LeeShin2016 would predict sharp dynamic-treatment panel bounds from stationarity restrictions. Our seed conjectures that reversible bounded-carryover bounds can be sharp without stationarity at finite T, because observed spell reversals provide local Lipschitz anchors for each history node.

Prior work DeChaisemartinDHaultfoeuille2024 would imply that nonabsorbing treatment lags can be handled in a DID/event-study design under parallel trends. Our seed conjectures that without parallel trends the same nonabsorbing lags define a sharp partial-ID interval, because lag contamination is a bounded latent response coordinate rather than a regression coefficient.

Prior work SunAbraham2021 would predict event-study contamination from heterogeneous effects in absorbing adoption designs. Our seed conjectures that reversible spells produce a different contamination object, because treatment endings and re-entries introduce two-sided spell-boundary distances that cannot be compressed to cohort event time.

Prior work CallawaySantAnna2021 would predict that group-time ATT identification relies on adoption-date state compression. Our seed conjectures that reversible repeated spells break that compression and require a support-function interval over recent spell histories, because a unit can leave treatment and return with residual carryover.

Prior work Han2021 would predict that finite dynamic treatment-sequence effects can be identified under dynamic-treatment assumptions. Our seed conjectures that the long-run reversible-spell target exists as the Cesaro limit of finite-horizon sharp intervals under finite-memory ergodicity, because the lower/upper g-recursions become Markov reward equations.

Prior work HanYang2024 would predict that dynamic policy-effect identification with unobserved heterogeneity is naturally computational. Our seed conjectures that reversible bounded-carryover sustained-regime effects admit analytic closed-form envelopes, because the scalar spillover radius turns the latent optimization into a finite Lipschitz extension problem.

Prior work KalouptsidiKitamuraLimaSouzaRodrigues2020 would predict scalar welfare bounds through constrained optimization in structural dynamic discrete-choice models. Our seed conjectures that a nonstructural reversible-spell long-run ATE has closed-form support-function endpoints, because the only latent restriction is a metric carryover radius on treatment histories.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work Robins1986 would predict that dynamic sustained-regime means are point-identified by the g-formula under full longitudinal assumptions. Our seed conjectures that a scalar adjacent-spell Lipschitz envelope is the sharp partial-identification bridge away from that point model, because reversible on-off spells create a finite metric-extension problem rather than an absorbing-regime recursion.

anchor_paper.author: Robins
anchor_paper.claim: Introduces the g-formula for time-varying treatments under sequential exchangeability, the zero-spillover point-identification endpoint.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Boundary/regime-shift pattern, not blocked: the proposed change is not just weakening sequential exchangeability; it predicts a new metric-extension sharpness formula with Robins and Manski as endpoints.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from Robins1986 because the g-formula already handles arbitrary treatment histories.
seed_details.rebuttal: False: Robins point-identifies under full longitudinal assumptions, while this seed asks for sharp partial-ID when only scalar adjacent-spell carryover is bounded.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work DiemerShiSwanson2024 would imply that assumption-free sustained dynamic intervention bounds remain wide when latent histories are unrestricted. Our seed conjectures that bounded adjacent-spell carryover strictly nests those bounds, because repeated reversible spells create observed support points that act as Lipschitz anchors for off-path histories.

anchor_paper.author: Diemer, Shi, Swanson
anchor_paper.claim: Extends assumption-free partial-identification bounds from time-fixed exposures to sustained static and dynamic interventions, emphasizing wide unrestricted bounds.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Weakening/strengthening pattern, not blocked: the scalar radius creates a phase path from unrestricted Manski bounds to point ID and predicts strict tightening from reversible support anchors.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from DiemerShiSwanson2024 because bounded outcomes already give sustained-intervention partial-ID intervals.
seed_details.rebuttal: False: their unrestricted dynamic bounds do not impose adjacent-spell Lipschitz geometry or give closed-form nested intervals indexed by a spillover radius.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work ChernozhukovFernandezValHahnNewey2013 would predict sharp panel bounds from time-homogeneity and state-dependence restrictions. Our seed conjectures that sharpness can instead be certified by McShane-Whitney extremal laws on a spell-boundary metric, because the binding faces are metric-extension faces rather than stationarity faces.

anchor_paper.author: Chernozhukov, Fernandez-Val, Hahn, Newey
anchor_paper.claim: Provides sharp bounds for average and quantile effects in nonseparable panel models and dynamic bounds for state dependence under time-homogeneity assumptions.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Mechanism-decomposition pattern, not blocked: the surprise is a different sharpness mechanism, metric extension rather than time-homogeneity.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from ChernozhukovFernandezValHahnNewey2013 because they already prove sharp panel bounds with dynamic state dependence.
seed_details.rebuttal: False: their faces come from panel time-homogeneity/nonseparability restrictions, not scalar bounded spillovers across reversible treatment spells.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work LeeShin2016 would predict sharp dynamic-treatment panel bounds from stationarity restrictions. Our seed conjectures that reversible bounded-carryover bounds can be sharp without stationarity at finite T, because observed spell reversals provide local Lipschitz anchors for each history node.

anchor_paper.author: Lee and Shin
anchor_paper.claim: Gives sharp identifiable bounds for potential-outcome distributions in panel data with dynamic treatment decisions using stationarity restrictions.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Assumption-inversion pattern, not blocked: finite-T sharpness is predicted to come from reversible-spell metric anchors, not from stationarity relaxation alone.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from LeeShin2016 because stationarity sharp bounds can be specialized to binary reversible treatments.
seed_details.rebuttal: False: specializing stationarity bounds does not yield the scalar rho path or endpoint collapse to Robins and Manski regimes.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work DeChaisemartinDHaultfoeuille2024 would imply that nonabsorbing treatment lags can be handled in a DID/event-study design under parallel trends. Our seed conjectures that without parallel trends the same nonabsorbing lags define a sharp partial-ID interval, because lag contamination is a bounded latent response coordinate rather than a regression coefficient.

anchor_paper.author: de Chaisemartin and d'Haultfoeuille
anchor_paper.claim: Studies non-binary, non-absorbing panel treatments with treatment lags, but under a parallel-trends design rather than sharp partial identification.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Extends method from DID to partial-ID, not blocked because the lag object changes from a regression estimand to a sharp latent-coordinate interval with endpoint laws.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from DeChaisemartinDHaultfoeuille2024 because they already allow nonabsorbing treatments and lags.
seed_details.rebuttal: False: their identifying variation is parallel trends and DID weights, while the seed is nonparametric partial ID with no parallel-trends restriction.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work SunAbraham2021 would predict event-study contamination from heterogeneous effects in absorbing adoption designs. Our seed conjectures that reversible spells produce a different contamination object, because treatment endings and re-entries introduce two-sided spell-boundary distances that cannot be compressed to cohort event time.

anchor_paper.author: Sun and Abraham
anchor_paper.claim: Shows contamination of event-study coefficients in staggered absorbing adoption when treatment effects are heterogeneous across periods.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Extension from absorbing to reversible, not blocked because reversals add new spell-ending and re-entry coordinates absent in absorbing event time.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from SunAbraham2021 because heterogeneous dynamic effects already contaminate event-study coefficients.
seed_details.rebuttal: False: their target is regression contamination under absorbing adoption, not sharp partial-ID of sustained-regime means under bounded carryover.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work CallawaySantAnna2021 would predict that group-time ATT identification relies on adoption-date state compression. Our seed conjectures that reversible repeated spells break that compression and require a support-function interval over recent spell histories, because a unit can leave treatment and return with residual carryover.

anchor_paper.author: Callaway and Sant'Anna
anchor_paper.claim: Develops group-time ATT identification for multiple-period DID in staggered adoption settings, relying on adoption-date state compression.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Extension from absorbing to reversible, not blocked because state compression provably fails when spell exit and re-entry histories carry latent response coordinates.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from CallawaySantAnna2021 because group-time effects can be indexed by treatment history instead of cohort.
seed_details.rebuttal: False: indexing by history does not supply sharp bounds or extremal latent laws under bounded spillover contamination.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work Han2021 would predict that finite dynamic treatment-sequence effects can be identified under dynamic-treatment assumptions. Our seed conjectures that the long-run reversible-spell target exists as the Cesaro limit of finite-horizon sharp intervals under finite-memory ergodicity, because the lower/upper g-recursions become Markov reward equations.

anchor_paper.author: Han
anchor_paper.claim: Identifies average potential outcomes for finite treatment sequences and emphasizes dynamic complications such as nonstationarity, state dependence, and timing.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Finite-T to long-run extension, not blocked because the extension exposes a new limit/interchange boundary for sharp intervals rather than just adding periods.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from Han2021 because finite treatment-sequence means imply their long-run averages by summation.
seed_details.rebuttal: False: finite-horizon identification does not characterize Hausdorff convergence of sharp partial-ID intervals under reversible bounded carryover.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work HanYang2024 would predict that dynamic policy-effect identification with unobserved heterogeneity is naturally computational. Our seed conjectures that reversible bounded-carryover sustained-regime effects admit analytic closed-form envelopes, because the scalar spillover radius turns the latent optimization into a finite Lipschitz extension problem.

anchor_paper.author: Han and Yang
anchor_paper.claim: Provides computational identification methods for treatment-effect policy evaluation with unobserved heterogeneity rather than closed-form long-run reversible-spell formulas.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Computational-to-analytic transformation, not blocked because the proposed phenomenon is closed-form metric-envelope collapse of a latent optimization problem.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from HanYang2024 because computational identification can solve the same finite optimization.
seed_details.rebuttal: False: numerical optimization does not give the closed-form rho-indexed nested formulas, endpoint reductions, or reusable sharpness witnesses.

seed_details.one_line: Prior work KalouptsidiKitamuraLimaSouzaRodrigues2020 would predict scalar welfare bounds through constrained optimization in structural dynamic discrete-choice models. Our seed conjectures that a nonstructural reversible-spell long-run ATE has closed-form support-function endpoints, because the only latent restriction is a metric carryover radius on treatment histories.

anchor_paper.author: Kalouptsidi, Kitamura, Lima, Souza-Rodrigues
anchor_paper.claim: Develops sharp bounds for structural dynamic discrete-choice counterfactuals with scalar welfare endpoints computed through constrained optimization.

seed_details.anti_pattern_check: Cross-setting extension, not blocked because the nonstructural metric restriction is predicted to remove structural optimization and produce explicit support functions.
seed_details.referee_objection: This is immediate from KalouptsidiKitamuraLimaSouzaRodrigues2020 because dynamic counterfactual bounds are already constrained optimization problems.
seed_details.rebuttal: False: their structural dynamic discrete-choice setup is not a closed-form reversible-spell partial-ID formula for panel treatment histories.

proposed_from.literature_map:
Robins (1986) is the zero-spillover point-identification anchor: the g-formula identifies dynamic intervention means under sequential exchangeability, positivity, and consistency.
Murphy (2003) and the dynamic treatment-regime literature refine optimal regimes but largely retain point-identifying longitudinal assumptions.
Han (2021, Journal of Econometrics) studies nonparametric identification of finite dynamic treatment sequences and highlights nonstationarity, state dependence, timing, and initial-condition complications.
Han (2024, JASA) studies partial welfare ordering for optimal dynamic regimes using binary instruments; no exact web hit was found for the named Han-Yao-Hsu (2024) string.
Heckman, Humphries, and Veramendi (2016, Journal of Econometrics) separate direct effects from continuation values in multistage choices, which is relevant when reversible spells interact.
Sun and Abraham (2021) and Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021) solve major absorbing-treatment event-study and DID identification problems, but their state variable is adoption timing.
de Chaisemartin and d'Haultfoeuille (2024, Review of Economics and Statistics) explicitly allow non-absorbing treatments and lags, but under parallel-trends structure rather than sharp partial identification.
Imai, Kim, and Wang (2023) and Liu, Wang, and Xu (2025) show practical reversible-panel methods using treatment-history matching, imputation, and no-carryover diagnostics.
Chernozhukov, Fernandez-Val, Hahn, and Newey (2013, Econometrica) and Lee and Shin (2016, JBES) provide sharp panel bounds with dynamic decisions or state dependence, but not scalar bounded spillovers between reversible spells.
Diemer, Shi, and Swanson (2024, Epidemiology) provide assumption-free dynamic sustained-intervention bounds, showing the unrestricted endpoint can be very wide.
Kalouptsidi, Kitamura, Lima, Souza-Rodrigues (2020, NBER) and Han and Yang (2024, Journal of Econometrics) represent computational partial-identification approaches rather than closed-form spillover-indexed formulas.
The live gap is a reversible-regime, bounded-carryover sharp partial-identification result that analytically connects Robins point identification to unrestricted Manski-style bounds and includes sharp extremal completions.

The epsilon-gap is an iff finite-window endpoint-diameter frontier for reversible-spell long-run sharp bounds, and a named warning that horizon-by-horizon sharp endpoints cannot be summarized by one stationary interval when endpoint cycling persists."},"literature_checklist":[{"author":"Han","year":2021,"venue":"Journal of Econometrics","bibkey":"Han2021","claim":"Identifies finite dynamic treatment-sequence effects under nonparametric restrictions, without a reversible-spell long-run sharp-set convergence criterion.","relevant_to":"Conjecture 1"},{"author":"Chernozhukov, Fernandez-Val, Hahn, Newey","year":2013,"venue":"Econometrica","bibkey":"ChernozhukovFernandezValHahnNewey2013","claim":"Gives sharp panel bounds under time-homogeneity/state-dependence restrictions rather than a finite-horizon-to-stationary frontier.","relevant_to":"Conjecture 1"},{"author":"Jun, Lee, Shin","year":2016,"venue":"Journal of Business and Economic Statistics","bibkey":"JunLeeShin2016","claim":"Provides sharp panel bounds for dynamic treatment decisions under stationarity, a direct imposed-stationarity comparator.","relevant_to":"Conjecture 1"},{"author":"Schneider","year":1993,"venue":"Cambridge University Press","bibkey":"Schneider1993","claim":"Supplies generic convex-body support-function continuity, but not causal latent-law liftability or observable endpoint diagnostics.","relevant_to":"Conjecture 1"},{"author":"Rockafellar and Wets","year":1998,"venue":"Springer","bibkey":"RockafellarWets1998","claim":"Provides variational convergence tools for compact sets, but not reversible-spell sharp causal compatibility.","relevant_to":"Conjecture 1"},{"author":"Kalouptsidi, Kitamura, Lima, Souza-Rodrigues","year":2020,"venue":"NBER Working Paper","bibkey":"KalouptsidiKitamuraLimaSouzaRodrigues2020","claim":"Computes finite dynamic counterfactual sharp bounds; banked_reason: Stage -0.5 NO-PASS: 3 angles capped at field/letter tier; proposal didn't anchor on a cited open-problem statement so the reviewer never agreed the topic clears flagship novelty.
